source.title: High-throughput screening of catalytically active inclusion bodies using laboratory automation and Bayesian optimization

initial_request.research_problem: No a priori prediction possible for best linker/tag combination for CatIBs, necessitating extensive screening.
initial_request.known_context: Catalytically active inclusion bodies (CatIBs) retain substantial catalytic activity but their formation and activity depend on the combination of aggregation-inducing tag, linker length/type, and N-/C-terminal fusion, with no general design rules.
constraints: High-throughput screening required due to large combinatorial space
Manual screening is labor-intensive (approx. 40 h for 48 variants)
input_data: 63 BsGDH-CatIB variants designed with different tags, linkers, and fusion orientations
quantifiable_goals: Identify best performing CatIB variant with >95% probability

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No a priori prediction possible for best linker/tag combination for CatIBs, necessitating extensive screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which combination of aggregation-inducing tag, linker, and fusion orientation yields the best CatIB performance for BsGDH?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A semi-automated cloning workflow can rapidly generate a large library of CatIB variants for screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement a semi-automated cloning workflow using Golden Gate Assembly to generate 63 BsGDH-CatIB variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Semi-automated cloning workflow implemented for fast generation of 63 CatIB variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Cloning failures observed for some variants, requiring investigation.

evidence.quote_or_span: no a priori prediction is possible so far to indicate the best combination thereof. Consequently, extensive screening is required to find the best performing CatIB variant.
a semi-automated cloning workflow was implemented and used for fast generation of 63 CatIB variants
For the generation of 96 CatIB variants only 11 h of manual work are needed
Due to the successful automation of the CatIB screening, which enables high-throughput testing in the context of DBTL cycles, the workflow could be applied to analyze all 63 BsGDH-CatIB variants.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Some variants may not be clonable due to non-viable phenotypes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Failed cloning attempts indicate non-viable phenotypes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Document cloning failures and proceed with successfully cloned variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 13 CatIB variants could not be cloned within three attempts, potentially due to non-viable phenotype.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to cultivation of successfully cloned variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: 13 CatIB variants could not be successfully cloned within three attempts, potentially due to a non-viable phenotype.
a semi-automated cloning workflow was implemented and used for fast generation of 63 CatIB variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Successfully cloned CatIB variants ready for cultivation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Systematic positional bias observed in BioLector backscatter values during cultivation at 15°C.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Observe and document the positional bias.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Systematic positional bias observed in BioLector backscatter values during cultivation at 15°C, with backscatter ranging from 60 to 80 a.u., mean 74.9 a.u., SD 4.5 a.u.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesize that temperature gradient from cooling fan caused bias.

evidence.quote_or_span: a systematic bias over the positions in the BioLector, which was observed during the second cultivation step at 15 °C
a systematic bias over the positions in the BioLector, which was observed during the second cultivation step at 15 °C
the final backscatter values across the FlowerPlate varied from approximately 60 to 80 a.u. with a mean and standard deviation of 74.9 a.u. and 4.5 a.u. respectively

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Temperature gradient from cooling fan causes positional bias.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Formulate hypothesis about the cause of bias.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesized that temperature gradient from cooling fan caused positional bias.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test hypothesis by changing cultivation temperature.

evidence.quote_or_span: The first challenge that was noted was a systematic bias over the positions in the BioLector, which was observed during the second cultivation step at 15 °C

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesized that temperature gradient causes bias.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Changing cultivation temperature to constant 25°C will eliminate positional bias.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cultivation temperature changed to 25°C for the whole process, excluding the cooling step.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that bias is eliminated and CatIB formation/activity is maintained.

evidence.quote_or_span: changing the cultivation temperature
the cultivation temperature was changed to 25 °C for the whole process, excluding the cooling step

evolution_trajectory.observation: Positional bias eliminated; backscatter standard deviation reduced from 4.5 to 1.1 a.u.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate CatIB formation and activity at 25°C.

evidence.quote_or_span: the positional bias could thus be prevented by using 25 °C as cultivation temperature
the final backscatter values across the FlowerPlate varied from approximately 60 to 80 a.u. with a mean and standard deviation of 74.9 a.u. and 4.5 a.u. respectively

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does 25°C cultivation maintain CatIB formation and enzymatic activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: CatIB formation and enzymatic activity are maintained at 25°C.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate CatIB formation and activity at 25°C.
evolution_trajectory.observation: CatIB formation and enzymatic activity confirmed at 25°C; some variants showed improved formation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize enzymatic assay for online measurement.

evidence.quote_or_span: the results indicate that cultivation at 25 °C overall has a positive influence on the CatIB formation rate for the BsGDH case study

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The optimized workflow will show high reproducibility.
evolution_trajectory.observation: High reproducibility achieved with 1.9% RSD after 120 min, compared to 11.4% previously.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Integrate validated workflow with Bayesian optimization for iterative screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: high reproducibility with 1.9% relative standard deviation across 42 biological replicates

a high reproducibility of the new workflow was demonstrated with 42 biological replicates, lowering the relative standard deviation of fluorescence signals after 120 min to only 1.9% compared to 11.4% in a previous study

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to efficiently screen 63 variants to identify the best performer?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining the automated workflow with a Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling will enable efficient iterative screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Integrate Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling for experimental design.

tool_or_method.name: Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling

evolution_trajectory.observation: Combined automated workflow with Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling for iterative screening of 63 variants in three batch experiments of 48 candidates each.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Execute iterative screening and analyze results.

evidence.quote_or_span: the workflow was performed in combination with a Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling
allowed analysis of 63 BsGDH-CatIB variants within only three batch experiments
the process model was used to quantify various experimental effects and their influence on the reaction rate of each CatIB variant in the final activity assay
we chose to model their reaction in the activity assay with a first order mass action law
this results in an overall manual work time of 7 h for 48 variants. This is approximately 12% of the operator workload for the typical manual procedure

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Three rounds of iterative screening completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which variant is the best performer?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Thompson sampling will identify the best variant with >95% probability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze results after three rounds; identify top candidate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Thompson sampling identified TDoT-PT-BsGDH as best performer with 98% probability after three rounds; inactive variants dropped early. Top four candidates had median reaction rate constants between 0.15 min^-1 and 0.19 min^-1.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate top candidate with a manual fourth round.

evidence.quote_or_span: Due to the probabilistic nature of the algorithm, two remaining variants, BsGDH-SG-L48KD, and CBDCell-PT-BsGDH, where not chosen in any round.
the top four candidates, TDoT-PT-BsGDH, BsGDH-PT-3HAMP, BsGDH-PT-CBDCell and BsGDH-PT-L6KD all have a median reaction rate constant between 0.15 min $^{-1}$ and 0.19 min $^{-1}$
Afterwards, TDoT-PT-BsGDH showed a 98% probability to be the best performer in the library (see Round 3), which exceed the termination criterion of $>95\%$ probability.

Due to the exploitation-exploration balancing, the Thompson sampling algorithm selected this variant in 50 biological replicates during the three screening rounds, leading to a statistically sound estimation of its reaction rate constant as the KPI for a library ranking.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TDoT-PT-BsGDH identified as top candidate with 98% probability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is TDoT-PT-BsGDH truly the best performer? Are unseen variants weak?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A manual fourth round will confirm TDoT-PT-BsGDH as best and unseen variants as weak.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conduct manual fourth round including previously unseen variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Manual fourth round confirmed TDoT-PT-BsGDH as best with >99% probability; unseen variants BsGDH-SG-L48KD and CBDCell-PT-BsGDH were weak performers.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Reflect on limitations of CatIB design rules.

evidence.quote_or_span: After the fourth round, it is clearly confirmed that TDoT-PT-BsGDH is the best performer in the library, which the algorithm determined with a probability of $>99\%$
the previously unseen variants BsGDH-SG-L48KD and CBDCell-PT-BsGDH did not lead to the successful formation of CatIBs and were weak performers not worth testing
After the fourth round, it is clearly confirmed that TDoT-PT-BsGDH is the best performer in the library, which the algorithm determined with a probability of $>99\%$ (Fig.
Afterwards, TDoT-PT-BsGDH showed a 98% probability to be the best performer in the library (see Round 3), which exceed the termination criterion of $>95\%$ probability.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why is the top performer an N-terminal fusion, contrary to expectations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The result exemplifies the limited understanding of structure-function relationship for CatIB design.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation: no general design rules can be derived.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Top performer TDoT-PT-BsGDH has N-terminal tag, exemplifying limited understanding of structure-function relationship for CatIB design.

evidence.quote_or_span: the top performer TDoT-PT-BsGDH is a variant with N-terminal addition of the tag, as such a perfect example of the still limited understanding of the structure–function relationship for CatIB design.
successful CatIB formation and activity highly depend on the combination of aggregation-inducing tag, length and type of the linker and N-/C-terminal fusion, allowing for no general conclusion

success_verification.final_conclusion: TDoT-PT-BsGDH identified as best CatIB variant with >99% probability; automated workflow reduced manual work by 88% and achieved 1.9% RSD.
limitations: Limited understanding of structure-function relationship for CatIB design; no general design rules can be derived.
13 variants could not be cloned, potentially due to non-viable phenotypes.
